1: theme It is good to give thanks to the LORD, to sing praises to your name, O Most High; The Sabbath psalm's opening — Christ is Lord of the Sabbath (Matthew 12:8), and the rest he provides is the true Sabbath (Hebrews 4:9-11). Giving thanks through Christ: through him then let us continually offer up a sacrifice of praise to God (Hebrews 13:15). The name Most High is the name Jesus bears (Mark 5:7 — Son of the Most High God).
2: theme to declare your steadfast love in the morning, and your faithfulness by night, Morning and night praise — the ceaseless doxology. Christ models this: night prayers on the mountain (Luke 6:12), morning rising to pray (Mark 1:35). His steadfast love (hesed) declared is the gospel itself: God shows his love for us in that while we were still sinners, Christ died for us (Romans 5:8).
3: theme to the music of the lute and the harp, to the melody of the lyre. Musical praise — the worship of the new covenant community gathered around Christ. Ephesians 5:19: addressing one another in psalms and hymns and spiritual songs, singing and making melody to the Lord with your heart. The Spirit-filled church takes up the instruments of the Psalter and directs them at the risen Christ.
4: theme For you, O LORD, have made me glad by your work; at the works of your hands I sing for joy. Gladness in God's works — the works of Christ producing joy: these things I have spoken to you, that my joy may be in you, and that your joy may be full (John 15:11). The disciples' gladness when they see the risen Lord (John 20:20) is the paradigm — the works of the Lord's hands (the resurrection) are the source of inexpressible joy.
5: revelation of God How great are your works, O LORD! Your thoughts are very deep! The greatness of God's works and the depth of his thoughts — Paul erupts in the same doxology after explaining the mystery of Christ and the church: Oh, the depth of the riches and wisdom and knowledge of God! How unsearchable are his judgments and how inscrutable his ways! (Romans 11:33). The deep thoughts of God are the wisdom that is Christ himself (1 Corinthians 1:24).
6: theme The stupid man cannot know; the fool cannot understand this: The fool who cannot understand — the natural man who cannot receive the things of the Spirit of God (1 Corinthians 2:14). The wisdom of the cross is foolishness to those who are perishing (1 Corinthians 1:18) — the depth of God's works in Christ is hidden from the foolish and revealed to babes (Matthew 11:25).
7: theme that though the wicked sprout like grass and all evildoers flourish, they are doomed to destruction forever, The flourishing wicked doomed to destruction — the eschatological reversal. Christ's parable of wheat and tares (Matthew 13:24-30): the tares flourish alongside the wheat until the harvest, when they are gathered for burning. The apparent prosperity of the wicked is temporary; the doom is certain and forever.
8: revelation of God but you, O LORD, are on high forever. The eternal height of God — fulfilled in the Ascension of Christ: he ascended far above all the heavens, that he might fill all things (Ephesians 4:10). The one who is on high forever is the same Christ who descended to earth's depths and rose to fill all things. The eternal highness is the highness of the crucified and risen Lord.
9: shadow For behold, your enemies, O LORD, for behold, your enemies shall perish; all evildoers shall be scattered. The perishing of God's enemies — fulfilled ultimately at the parousia. He must reign until he has put all his enemies under his feet. The last enemy to be destroyed is death (1 Corinthians 15:25-26). The scattering of evildoers begins at the cross (the powers disarmed, Colossians 2:15) and completes at the last day.
10: shadow But you have exalted my horn like that of the wild ox; you have poured over me fresh oil. Exalted horn and fresh oil — the anointing of the Messiah. Christ = Anointed One; the Spirit descends on him like oil at his baptism (Matthew 3:16; Isaiah 61:1 — the Spirit of the Lord is upon me, because he has anointed me). God exalts his horn through the resurrection and enthronement (Philippians 2:9-11).
11: shadow My eyes have seen the downfall of my enemies; my ears have heard the doom of my evil assailants. Seeing the downfall of enemies — the vindicated righteous one's perspective. Christ sees the defeat of his enemies from his enthronement at the Father's right hand: the Lord said to my Lord, Sit at my right hand, until I make your enemies your footstool (Psalms 110:1; Matthew 22:44). He waits from the position of victory already secured.
12: shadow The righteous flourish like the palm tree and grow like a cedar in Lebanon. The righteous flourishing — the fruitful branches abiding in Christ: I am the vine; you are the branches. Whoever abides in me and I in him, he it is that bears much fruit (John 15:5). The palm and cedar are the imagery of permanence and dignity; those in Christ grow with the stability of deep-rooted trees.
13: shadow They are planted in the house of the LORD; they flourish in the courts of our God. Planted in God's house — rooted and grounded in love (Ephesians 3:17); planted in Christ who is the true temple (John 2:21). The courts of God are now the gathering of the church around the risen Christ, in whom the fullness of the Godhead dwells bodily (Colossians 2:9).
14: shadow They still bear fruit in old age; they are ever full of sap and green, Fruitful in old age — the eternal fruitfulness of those in Christ who drink from the living water: the water that I will give him will become in him a spring of water welling up to eternal life (John 4:14). The green, sap-full tree is the believer permanently rooted in Christ, bearing fruit that remains (John 15:16).
15: shadow to declare that the LORD is upright; he is my rock, and there is no unrighteousness in him. The upright rock with no unrighteousness — Christ: in him there is no sin (1 John 3:5); no deceit was found in his mouth (1 Peter 2:22; Isaiah 53:9). The rock is Christ (1 Corinthians 10:4). The declaration of God's uprightness is the declaration of Christ's righteousness — the foundation on which the justified sinner stands.
